title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct

title: Data Generator
text: data_generator function enables users generate two multivariate normal samples varying sizes, whose number features/dimensions equal. two samples representative two experimental conditions one typically determine differential network. Due technicalities differential network estimation, possible validate accuracy estimates obtained use traditional statistical methodologies. data_generator aims address generating data whose covariance matrices, precision matrices known -priori differential network. allows users validate estimation procedure specific experimental criteria.

title: Data Generator
text: important arguments available within data_generator follows:
code: # Number of observations in sample 1 n_X <- 100   # Number of observations in sample 2 n_Y <- 150  # Number of features in each of the samples p <- 50  # The form of the precision covariance matrices case <- "sparse"  # The seed of the simulation process to ensure reproducibility of results seed <- 123

title: Data Generator
text: defined key arguments , data_generator function can now called:
code: data <- data_generator(n_X = n_X, n_Y = n_Y, p = p, case = case, seed = seed)

what: Switching Between Sequential and Parallel Mode
title: Parallelisation
text: cores argument change required move modes. value must positive integer greater parallel::detectCores() - 1. Sequential (default, cores = 1): Parallel (cores set desired number workers): calls return identical results — cores affects computation time, estimates output field.
code: # Check how many cores are available on your machine parallel::detectCores() # Generate data for the examples below data <- data_generator(n_X = 150, p = 100, seed = 42) X <- data$X Y <- data$Y result_seq <- estimation(X, Y, nlambda = 15, cores = 1) result_seq$elapse  # elapsed time in seconds result_par <- estimation(X, Y, nlambda = 15, cores = 4) result_par$elapse  # elapsed time in seconds

title: Parallelisation
text: table reports wall-clock times speed-factors measured Apple M-series machine (8 logical cores) using 4 parallel workers. scenario uses default LASSO loss tuning. code used produce results provided reproducibility:
code: library(dineR)  run_bench <- function(label, n_X, p, nlambda, cores_par) {   data <- data_generator(n_X = n_X, p = p, seed = 42)   X <- data$X; Y <- data$Y    r_seq <- estimation(X, Y, nlambda = nlambda, cores = 1)   r_par <- estimation(X, Y, nlambda = nlambda, cores = cores_par)    cat(sprintf(     "[%s] seq: %.3fs | par(%d cores): %.3fs | speed-up: %.2fx\n",     label, r_seq$elapse, cores_par, r_par$elapse,     r_seq$elapse / r_par$elapse   )) }  run_bench("Small",    n_X = 100, p = 50,  nlambda = 10, cores_par = 4) run_bench("Medium",   n_X = 150, p = 100, nlambda = 15, cores_par = 4) run_bench("Large",    n_X = 200, p = 150, nlambda = 20, cores_par = 4) run_bench("High-dim", n_X = 100, p = 200, nlambda = 20, cores_par = 4) run_bench("Many-lam", n_X = 150, p = 100, nlambda = 30, cores_par = 4)

title: Parallelisation
text: Several patterns emerge benchmarks: Small problems (p = 50, nlambda = 10): parallelisation provides benefit. overhead spawning worker processes distributing work exceeds per-lambda computation time. Sequential execution preferred . Medium--large problems (p ≥ 100 nlambda ≥ 15): consistent 2–3× speed-ups observed 4 cores. Gains driven dimensionality (p increases per-lambda solve time) number lambda values (nlambda increases number independent tasks can distributed). Speed-scales workload: largest absolute savings occur high-dimensional fine-grained regularisation paths, lambda solve computationally expensive.

title: Parallelisation
text: practical rule thumb: Use cores = 1 p < 100 nlambda < 15. Use cores = min(nlambda, parallel::detectCores() - 1) larger problems, reserving one core main R process. Note estimation automatically reduce cores nlambda cores requested lambda values, warn accordingly.
code: # Recommended cores selection for larger problems n_cores <- min(nlambda, parallel::detectCores() - 1) result <- estimation(X, Y, nlambda = nlambda, cores = n_cores)

title: Data Generating Function — data_generator
text: function generates two multivariate normal samples, means simulation. samples can represented follows: $$X \sim N_p(0, \Sigma_1)$$ $$Y \sim N_p(0, \Sigma_2)$$ restriction sample number features \(p\).

title: Data Generating Function — data_generator
code: data_generator(   n_X = NULL,   n_Y = NULL,   p = NULL,   Delta = NULL,   case = "sparse",   seed = NULL )

title: Data Generating Function — data_generator
text: n_X number observations generated first sample. n_Y number observations generated second sample. one sample sizes need specified. scenario, sample size sample set specified sample size. p dimensions/features samples. Delta Optional parameter - Provides differential network sample covariance matrices must derived. case Optional parameter - Allows specification precision matrix structure. Possible cases : "sparse" - Sparse Case "asymsparse"- Asymptotically Sparse Case. Defaults "sparse". Sparse Case: \(\Omega_1 = (0.5^{|-j|})^{-1}\). , \(\{\Omega_1\}_{1,1} = \{\Omega_1\}_{p,p} = \frac{4}{3}, \{\Omega_1\}_{,} = \frac{5}{3}\) \(\). \(\{\Omega_1\}_{, {+ 1}} = \{\Omega_1\}_{{-1},} = \frac{2}{3}\) \(\{\Omega_1\}_{, j} = 0\) \(, j\). Asymptotically Sparse Case seed Optional parameter - Allows seed set reproducibility.

title: Data Generating Function — data_generator
text: list various outputs, namely: case - case used. seed_option - seed used simulation. X - first multivariate normal sample. Y - second multivariate normal sample. n_X - number observations simulated X. n_Y - number observations simulated Y. Sigma_X - covariance matrix X: \(\Sigma_X\). Sigma_Y - covariance matrix Y: \(\Sigma_Y\). Omega_X - precision matrix X: \(\Sigma_X^{-1} = \Omega_X\). Omega_Y - precision matrix Y: \(\Sigma_Y^{-1} = \Omega_Y\). Diff_Omega - difference precision matrices: \(\Omega_X - \Omega_Y\). Delta - target differential network: \(\Delta\).

title: Data Generating Function — data_generator
text: Tang, Z., Yu, Z. Wang, C., 2020. fast iterative algorithm high-dimensional differential network. Computational Statistics, 35(1), pp.95-109.

title: Data Generating Function — data_generator
code: data <- data_generator(n_X = 100, p = 50, seed = 123) data <- data_generator(n_X = 10, p = 50, case = "asymsparse")

title: Estimation — estimation
text: function performs alternating direction method multipliers optimization variety loss functions estimate differential network given two samples multivariate normal data.

title: Estimation — estimation
code: estimation(   X,   Y,   lambdas = NULL,   lambda_min_ratio = 0.3,   nlambda = 10,   a = NULL,   loss = "lasso",   tuning = "none",   perturb = FALSE,   stop_tol = 1e-05,   max_iter = 500,   correlation = FALSE,   Delta_init = NULL,   rho = NULL,   gamma = NULL,   cores = 1,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Estimation — estimation
text: X first multivariate normal sample. Y second multivariate normal sample. lambdas Optional parameter - list regularization values used within loss functions. lambda_min_ratio Optional parameter - Defines smallest regularization values proportion largest regularization value. Defaults 0.3. nlambda Optional parameter - number regularization values considered. Defaults 10. Optional parameter - thresholding parameter used SCAD MCP loss functions. Defaults 3.7 SCAD, 3 MCP respectively. loss Optional parameter - loss function choice implement. function allows four choices, namely "lasso", "scad", "mcp" "d-trace". Defaults "lasso". tuning Optional parameter - tuning method selected determine optimal value regularization parameter. Options "none", "AIC", "BIC" "EBIC". Defaults "none". perturb Optional parameter - set TRUE perturbation done CLIME software improve performance implemented. Options TRUE FALSE, function defaulting FALSE. stop_tol Optional parameter - stop tolerance determine whether convergence occurred. Defaults 1e-5. max_iter Optional parameter - maximum number iterations can perform one regularization value. Defaults 100. correlation Optional parameter - Determines whether sample correlation matrices used place sample covariance matrices. Choices TRUE FALSE function defaulting FALSE. Delta_init Optional parameter - Allows algorithm provided initial estimate differential network ease computation. rho Optional parameter - Allows user adjust ADMM step-size. Defaults 1. gamma Optional parameter - Allows user adjust EBIC value EBIC selected tuning method. Defaults 0.5. cores Optional parameter - Allows user specify number cores used optimization. Defaults 1, .e sequential solving however appropriate values range 2 minimum number lambdas total number available cores. verbose Optional parameter - Allows user obtain summary estimation results. Options TRUE FALSE, FALSE indicates summary provided. Defaults FALSE.

title: Estimation — estimation
text: list various outputs, namely: n_X - number observations X. n_Y - number observations Y. Sigma_X - covariance matrix X. Sigma_Y - covariance matrix Y. loss - loss function implemented. tuning - tuning method utilized. lip - value lipschitz constant. iter - iterations convergence regularization values. elapse - total system time (seconds) elapsed initialization completion optimization. lambdas - regularization parameter values used. sparsity - level sparsity differential network regularization value. path - set differential networks regularization values considered. ic - output obtained possible tuning. ic_index - index tuning optimized. ic_value - tuning method optimal value. chosen_lambda_ic - regularization value occurs ic_index. loss_index - index loss function optimized. loss_value - loss function optimal value. chosen_lambda_loss - regularization value occurs loss_index.

title: Estimation — estimation
text: Boyd, S., Parikh, N., Chu, E., Peleato, B. Eckstein, J., 2011. Distributed optimization statistical learning via alternating direction method multipliers. Foundations Trends® Machine learning, 3(1), pp.1-122. Chen, J. Chen, Z., 2008. Extended Bayesian information criteria model selection large model spaces. Biometrika, 95(3), pp.759-771. Friedman, J., Hastie, T. Tibshirani, R., 2008. Sparse inverse covariance estimation graphical lasso. Biostatistics, 9(3), pp.432-441. Tang, Z., Yu, Z. Wang, C., 2020. fast iterative algorithm high-dimensional differential network. Computational Statistics, 35(1), pp.95-109. Yuan, H., Xi, R., Chen, C. Deng, M., 2017. Differential network analysis via lasso penalized D-trace loss. Biometrika, 104(4), pp.755-770. Zhang, T. Zou, H., 2014. Sparse precision matrix estimation via lasso penalized D-trace loss. Biometrika, 101(1), pp.103-120.

title: NPN - Non paranormal Transformation — npn
text: functions allows us transform non-normal multivariate data non paranormal data.

title: NPN - Non paranormal Transformation — npn
code: npn(x, npn_func = "shrinkage", npn_thresh = NULL, verbose = TRUE)

title: NPN - Non paranormal Transformation — npn
text: x multivariate non-normal data transformed. npn_func Optional parameter - method transformation applied. Can either "shrinkage" "truncation" defaults "shrinkage". npn_thresh Optional parameter - truncation threshold used making use truncation. verbose Optional parameter - Prints additional output selected approach. Can either "TRUE" "FALSE" defaults "TRUE".

title: NPN - Non paranormal Transformation — npn
text: Returns transformed data matrix.

title: NPN - Non paranormal Transformation — npn
text: Liu, H., Han, F., Yuan, M., Lafferty, J. Wasserman, L., 2012. nonparanormal skeptic. arXiv preprint arXiv:1206.6488. Liu, H., Lafferty, J. Wasserman, L., 2009. nonparanormal: Semiparametric estimation high dimensional undirected graphs. Journal Machine Learning Research, 10(10). Xue, L. Zou, H., 2012. Regularized rank-based estimation high-dimensional nonparanormal graphical models. Annals Statistics, 40(5), pp.2541-2571.

title: NPN - Non paranormal Transformation — npn
code: data <- data_generator(n_X = 100, p = 50, seed = 123) X <- data$X X_transformed <- npn(X, npn_func = "truncation") #> Nonparanomral transformation via truncated ECDF.

title: dineR 2.0.0
text: estimation() now supports parallel execution across regularisation path via cores argument. Setting cores > 1 distributes lambda value across worker processes using doSNOW, yielding 2–3× speed-ups medium--large problems (p ≥ 100 nlambda ≥ 15). Sequential execution (cores = 1) remains default. data_generator() now accepts asymmetric sample sizes: n_Y can specified independently n_X, allowing two samples different numbers observations. differential network matrices returned estimation() now stored sparse matrices (dgCMatrix class via Matrix package), reducing memory usage high-dimensional problems.

title: dineR 2.0.0
text: Parallelisation — covers switch sequential parallel modes, documents benchmark results across five problem sizes, provides guidance choosing number cores. Estimation — step--step walkthrough data generation estimation workflow. Data Generator — documents data_generator() function outputs detail. Differential Networks — end--end tutorial generating data estimating differential network.

title: dineR 2.0.0
text: Fixed summary.estimation() S3 method signature match summary generic (object, ...), resolving R CMD check warning. Fixed partial argument matching ambiguity data_generator() n matched n_X n_Y. Added missing @importFrom foreach foreach %dopar% directive, resolving undefined global variable notes R CMD check. Added Matrix foreach Imports doParallel Suggests DESCRIPTION.
